name: ISO/IEC 27001:2013
description: International standard for information security management systems (ISMS). Predecessor to ISO/IEC 27001:2022, providing requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an ISMS with 114 Annex A controls.
businessImpact: Certification is a prerequisite for many public-sector contracts and regulated-industry procurement processes.
Requires documented policies, defined roles, regular internal audits and management reviews.
Surveillance audits can suspend or withdraw certification on non-conformity, making continual improvement mandatory.
Transition to the 2022 edition was required by October 2025, imposing gap analysis and recertification costs.
structure: 10 mandatory clauses following the PDCA cycle (Plan–Do–Check–Act).
Clauses 4–10: Context, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement.
Clause 6 requires a formal risk assessment methodology and a Statement of Applicability (SoA).
Annex A: 114 controls in 14 categories (A.5–A.18) covering policies, HR, asset management, cryptography, operations, and compliance.
Aligned with ISO High-Level Structure for integration with ISO 9001, ISO 22301 and other management standards.

name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022
description: International standard for information security management systems (ISMS). Provides requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an ISMS.
businessImpact: Global benchmark for information security; increasingly required in procurement across finance, healthcare and cloud services.
11 new controls added in 2022 covering threat intelligence, cloud security, data masking and secure development.
Annual surveillance audits and a full recertification cycle every three years embed continual improvement.
Organisations certified under the 2013 edition must transition by October 2025: gap analysis, updated docs, retraining and new audit required.
structure: 10 mandatory clauses aligned with ISO's Harmonized Structure for easy integration with other ISO management standards.
Clauses 4–10: Context, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement.
Annex A restructured to 93 controls across 4 themes: Organisational (37), People (8), Physical (14), Technological (34).
Controls carry 5 attribute tags (type, security property, cybersecurity concept, capability, domain) to support cross-framework mapping.
ISO/IEC 27002:2022 provides detailed implementation guidance for each Annex A control.

name: ISO/IEC 27002:2022
description: Code of practice for information security controls providing implementation guidance for the 93 controls referenced in ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A. Serves as the companion guidance standard to ISO 27001.
businessImpact: Provides the detailed 'how-to' guidance for implementing all 93 controls listed in ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.
Restructured from 114 controls across 14 domains to 93 controls in 4 themes: Organisational, People, Physical, Technological.
11 new controls introduced in 2022 cover threat intelligence, ICT readiness, web filtering, secure coding and data masking.
Attribute tagging system (5 attributes per control) enables targeted gap analysis and cross-framework mapping.
Adoption supports ISO 27001 certification readiness; auditors use it as the primary reference for control implementation adequacy.
structure: 4 control themes: Organisational (37 controls), People (8 controls), Physical (14 controls), Technological (34 controls).
93 controls in total, each with: Control, Purpose, Guidance, and Other Information sections.
Each control tagged with 5 attributes: Control type, Information security property, Cybersecurity concept, Operational capability, Security domain.
Annex A: Attribute cross-reference tables for filtering controls by security property, concept or capability.
Cross-references to other ISO/IEC standards including 27001, 27005, 27017, 27018 and 27035.

name: ISO/IEC 27005:2022
description: International guidance standard for information security risk management. Supports implementation of the risk assessment and treatment requirements of ISO/IEC 27001 and aligns with ISO 31000.
businessImpact: Provides structured methodology for conducting the risk assessments mandated by ISO/IEC 27001 clauses 6.1.2 and 8.2.
Third edition (2022) aligns with ISO 31000:2018 and introduces event-based and asset-based risk assessment approaches.
Supports organisations in selecting risk treatment options and producing the Statement of Applicability (SoA).
Widely referenced in ISO 27001 certification audits to assess the rigour of the risk management process.
Applicable to all organisations regardless of size, sector or nature — guidance is adaptable to context.
structure: Core risk management process: risk identification, risk analysis, risk evaluation and risk treatment.
Two risk assessment approaches: event-based (threat-scenario driven) and asset-based (asset-threat-vulnerability driven).
Clauses aligned with ISO 31000 framework: establishing context, risk assessment, risk treatment, monitoring and review.
Annex A: Examples of risk identification techniques including threat catalogues and vulnerability sources.
Annex B: Risk treatment and residual risk acceptance guidance aligned with ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A controls.

name: ISO/IEC 27017:2015
description: Code of practice for information security controls based on ISO/IEC 27002 for cloud services. Provides cloud-specific implementation guidance and seven additional controls for cloud service providers and customers.
businessImpact: Applicable to both cloud service providers (CSPs) and cloud service customers (CSCs), clarifying shared-responsibility obligations.
7 cloud-specific controls address virtual machine hardening, cloud administrator monitoring, segregation in virtual environments and more.
Referenced in cloud procurement contracts and CSP compliance programmes (AWS, Azure, GCP all reference ISO 27017 in their compliance portfolios).
Supports alignment with GDPR Art. 28 (processor obligations) and ENISA cloud security guidelines.
Certification against ISO 27017 (in conjunction with ISO 27001) is increasingly demanded by enterprise cloud buyers.
structure: Extends ISO/IEC 27002:2013 with cloud-specific implementation guidance for each applicable control.

7 additional cloud controls: CLD.6.3.1 (shared roles), CLD.9.5.1 (virtual machine hardening), CLD.9.5.2 (VM lifecycle), CLD.12.1.5 (admin operations), CLD.12.4.5 (monitoring), CLD.13.1.4 (network alignment), CLD.13.2.6 (data return/deletion).
Guidance split by role: cloud service provider (CSP) and cloud service customer (CSC) perspectives for each control.
Annex A: Relationship between ISO/IEC 27017 and ISO/IEC 27002 control sets.
Cross-referenced with ISO/IEC 27018 for PII-specific cloud controls.

name: ISO/IEC 27018:2019
description: Code of practice for protection of personally identifiable information (PII) in public clouds acting as PII processors. Extends ISO/IEC 27002 with privacy-specific controls for cloud service providers.
businessImpact: Directly relevant to cloud service providers processing personal data on behalf of customers, particularly under GDPR Art. 28.
Provides a recognised compliance framework for demonstrating accountability as a data processor in public cloud environments.
Certification (as an extension to ISO 27001) demonstrates to cloud customers that their data is handled in accordance with privacy principles.
11 additional privacy-specific controls cover purposes, consent, transparency, data subject rights and PII deletion.
Microsoft Azure, AWS and Google Cloud reference ISO 27018 certification in their GDPR processor commitments.
structure: Extends ISO/IEC 27002 with privacy-specific implementation guidance and 11 additional PII-protection controls.
Covers 5 privacy principles: Consent and choice, Purpose legitimacy, Collection limitation, Data minimisation, Transparency.
PII-specific controls address: processing purpose limitation, prohibition on use for advertising/marketing without consent, deletion and return of PII.
Annex A: 25 public-cloud-specific PII controls mapped to ISO 29100 privacy principles.
Cross-referenced with ISO/IEC 27017 and ISO/IEC 27701 for cloud and privacy management integration.

name: ISO/IEC 27701:2019
description: Privacy Information Management System (PIMS) standard extending ISO/IEC 27001 and ISO/IEC 27002 to include privacy requirements. Provides a framework for managing personally identifiable information (PII) aligned with GDPR and other privacy regulations.
businessImpact: Extends existing ISO 27001 ISMS certification to cover privacy, reducing duplication of audit effort.
Provides a certifiable framework for demonstrating GDPR accountability, referenced by EU regulators and DPAs.
Separate guidance for PII controllers and PII processors — enables precise mapping to GDPR Art. 24/28 obligations.
Annex D maps 27701 requirements to GDPR articles, making gap analysis straightforward for EU-regulated organisations.
Increasingly requested by enterprise procurement teams and regulators as evidence of privacy maturity.
structure: Extends ISO/IEC 27001 clauses 4–10 with PIMS-specific requirements for context, planning and operations.
Extends ISO/IEC 27002 controls with additional privacy implementation guidance.
Part A: PIMS-specific requirements for PII controllers (additional to ISO 27001/27002 requirements).
Part B: PIMS-specific requirements for PII processors (additional to ISO 27001/27002 requirements).
Annex D: Mapping to GDPR articles for direct compliance gap analysis.
Annex F: Mapping to ISO 29151 (code of practice for PII protection).

name: NIS2 Directive
description: EU Directive on measures for a high common level of cybersecurity across the Union (2022/2555). Replaces the original NIS Directive and significantly expands its scope.
businessImpact: Covers entities in 18 critical sectors (energy, transport, banking, health, digital infrastructure, etc.) plus additional important sectors.
Applies by default to medium and large organisations; smaller entities are in scope where they are critical.
Management bodies are personally accountable; executives face temporary bans from management roles for repeated violations.
Fines up to €20 M or 4% of global turnover (essential entities) or €10 M / 2% (important entities).
Member states had until October 2024 to transpose; businesses operating across borders must track national implementations.
structure: 10 chapters, 46 articles.
Art. 21: 10 mandatory security areas — incident handling, BCP, supply chain security, vulnerability disclosure, cyber hygiene, cryptography, etc.
Art. 23: Three-stage incident reporting — early warning (24 h), notification (72 h), final report (1 month).
Chapters IV–V: Cross-border jurisdiction, national registration databases and EU coordination via NIS Cooperation Group, CSIRTs network and EU-CyCLONe.
Chapter VI: European cybersecurity certification framework.
Fully replaces the original NIS Directive.

name: General Data Protection Regulation
description: EU regulation on data protection and privacy (2016/679). Sets rules for the processing of personal data and ensures the protection of fundamental rights of individuals.
businessImpact: Extraterritorial scope: applies to any organisation processing EU residents' personal data, regardless of where it is established.
Requires DPO appointment (in certain cases), records of processing activities (ROPA), and DPIAs for high-risk processing.
72-hour supervisory authority breach notification obligation.
Fines up to €20 M or 4% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher.
International data transfers require a valid legal basis (adequacy decision, SCCs, BCRs) — continually scrutinised post-Schrems II.
structure: 11 chapters, 99 articles, 173 recitals providing interpretive guidance.

Ch. II: 6 lawful bases for processing + 7 data protection principles (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimisation, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity & confidentiality, accountability).
Ch. III: Data subject rights — access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection.
Ch. IV: Controller/processor obligations — contracts, ROPA, security measures, breach notification, DPIAs, DPO.
Ch. V: Rules for transfers to third countries or international organisations.
One-stop-shop mechanism: cross-border cases handled by the lead supervisory authority in the controller's main establishment.

name: Digital Operational Resilience Act
description: EU regulation on digital operational resilience for the financial sector (2022/2554). Harmonises ICT risk management requirements for financial entities across the EU.
businessImpact: Directly applicable across all EU member states from 17 January 2025 — no transposition divergence.
Covers 22,000+ EU financial entities: banks, insurers, investment firms, crypto-asset providers, payment institutions.
Mandatory ICT risk management framework, harmonised incident classification and tiered regulatory reporting.
Significant entities must conduct threat-led penetration testing (TLPT) every 3 years.
Critical ICT third-party providers (CTPPs) subject to direct European Supervisory Authority oversight.
Non-compliance can trigger supervisory sanctions; CTPPs may face suspension of services to financial entities.
structure: 9 chapters, 64 articles; supplemented by Level 2 RTS/ITS from EBA, EIOPA and ESMA.
Ch. II: ICT risk management framework — strategy, governance, continuous monitoring, BCM and disaster recovery.
Ch. III: Harmonised incident classification; three-phase reporting to competent authorities (initial, intermediate, final).
Ch. IV: Basic digital resilience testing for all entities; advanced TLPT for significant entities.
Ch. V: Third-party ICT risk — contractual requirements, exit strategies, concentration risk management.
Chs. VII–IX: CTPP oversight regime and transitional provisions.

name: CIS Controls v8
description: Prioritised set of actions to protect organisations and data from known cyber attack vectors. Developed by the Center for Internet Security.
businessImpact: Voluntary but widely referenced in cyber insurance underwriting, procurement requirements and regulatory guidance.
Three Implementation Groups (IG1–IG3) scale adoption proportionally to organisation size and risk profile.
IG1 covers essential cyber hygiene for small organisations; IG3 covers all 153 Safeguards for high-risk environments.
Built-in crosswalks to NIST CSF, ISO 27001, CMMC, HIPAA and PCI DSS reduce compliance duplication.
structure: 18 Controls and 153 Safeguards organised around asset types (enterprise assets, software, data, accounts, network, applications).
Controls 1–2: Asset inventory (hardware and software).
Controls 3–6: Data protection, secure configuration, account management, access control.
Controls 7–10: Vulnerability management, audit log management, email and web browser defences.
Controls 11–16: Network monitoring, malware defences, data recovery, network infrastructure, application security.
Controls 17–18: Incident response management and penetration testing.
Each Safeguard specifies asset type, security function and IG assignment for clear prioritisation.

name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
description: Voluntary framework for managing and reducing cybersecurity risk, organised around six core functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
businessImpact: De facto global reference architecture; effectively mandatory for US federal agencies.
Referenced in cyber insurance underwriting, M&A due diligence and sector-specific regulatory guidance.
Version 2.0 (Feb 2024) broadened scope from critical infrastructure to all organisations.
New Govern function places cybersecurity strategy, supply-chain risk and oversight at board/executive level.
Profile mechanism enables structured gap analysis between current and target security posture.
structure: 6 Core Functions: Govern (GV), Identify (ID), Protect (PR), Detect (DE), Respond (RS), Recover (RC).
22 Categories and 106 Subcategories with specific, measurable outcomes.
Tiers 1–4 (Partial → Risk Informed → Repeatable → Adaptive) describe risk governance maturity.
Profiles (Current / Target) support posture communication and investment prioritisation.
Comprehensive informative references mapping to ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, CIS Controls, COBIT and others.

name: KRITIS / IT-Sicherheitsgesetz 2.0
description: German regulation for operators of critical infrastructure (Kritische Infrastrukturen) under the BSI Act (BSIG) and IT Security Act 2.0 (IT-Sicherheitsgesetz 2.0, 2021). Mandates state-of-the-art technical and organisational security measures, attack detection systems, proof of compliance every two years, and incident reporting obligations for critical infrastructure operators.
businessImpact: Applies to operators in 9 critical sectors (energy, water, food, IT, transport, health, finance, media, waste) meeting threshold values defined in BSI-KritisV.
Must implement state-of-the-art security measures and deploy attack detection systems (§ 8a BSIG).
Biennial proof of compliance required via qualified audits, certifications or self-assessments submitted to the BSI.
24-hour incident reporting obligation to the BSI (§ 8b BSIG).
IT-SiG 2.0 introduced UNBÖFI (organisations of special public interest) with additional, lighter obligations.
Fines up to €20 M; untrusted ICT components can be excluded from critical systems by BSI order.
structure: Legal basis: BSI Act (BSIG) as amended by IT Security Act 2.0 (BGBl. I 2021, Nr. 25).
§ 2 BSIG: Definitions of critical infrastructure by sector and threshold value.
§ 8a BSIG: Security measures obligation + biennial compliance proof.
§ 8b BSIG: Point-of-contact registration + 24-hour incident reporting.
§ 8d BSIG: Obligations for organisations of special public interest (UNBÖFI).
BSI-KritisV: Sector-specific threshold values for 9 critical sectors.
BSI Orientierungshilfe: Guidance specifying minimum measures expected under § 8a.

name: NIS2-Umsetzungsgesetz (NIS2UmsuCG)
description: German national implementation of the EU NIS2 Directive (2022/2555). The NIS-2-Umsetzungs- und Cybersicherheitsstärkungsgesetz transposes the NIS2 requirements into German law, extends and replaces the existing IT Security Act framework, and introduces strengthened obligations for essential and important entities including management accountability and supply chain security.
businessImpact: Expected to affect ~29,000 organisations in Germany — a major expansion from ~1,600 under the KRITIS regime.
Both essential and important entities must register with the BSI and implement NIS2-aligned risk management measures.
24-hour initial notification and 72-hour detailed incident reporting obligations to the BSI.
Personal management liability: executives can be held personally liable and temporarily prohibited from management roles.
Fines: essential entities up to €10 M or 2% of global turnover; important entities up to €7 M or 1.4%.
Replaces and modernises the existing BSIG and IT-SiG 2.0 as a unified cybersecurity legal basis in Germany.
structure: Core: new BSIG version; also amends sector-specific laws (EnWG, TKG, etc.).
§ 28 BSIG-E: Definitions of essential and important entities by sector and size criteria.
§ 30 BSIG-E: 10 mandatory risk-management measure categories mirroring NIS2 Art. 21.
§ 32 BSIG-E: Tiered incident reporting to the BSI (initial, detailed, final).
§ 33 BSIG-E: Registration and point-of-contact designation.
§ 38 BSIG-E: Management body liability provisions.
§§ 60–65 BSIG-E: BSI enforcement powers (audits, sanctions).
Also transposes the EU CER Directive on physical resilience of critical entities.

name: BSI IT-Grundschutz
description: Comprehensive information security methodology published by Germany's Federal Office for Information Security (BSI). Consists of BSI Standards (200-1 through 200-4) and the IT-Grundschutz Compendium, providing building blocks for establishing and operating an ISMS. Widely used by German public authorities and recognised as equivalent to ISO/IEC 27001.
businessImpact: Dominant methodology in German public administration; widely adopted by public-sector suppliers and regulated utilities.
IT-Grundschutz certification is accepted as equivalent to ISO/IEC 27001.
Satisfies the KRITIS biennial proof-of-compliance requirement under § 8a BSIG.
German federal and state authorities are largely obliged to follow the methodology.
Modular pre-defined safeguards reduce the cost and effort of bespoke risk treatment planning.
Compendium updated annually — new building blocks for cloud, IoT and ICS require yearly applicability reviews.
structure: Four BSI Standards: 200-1 (ISMS, aligned with ISO 27001), 200-2 (Methodology), 200-3 (Risk Analysis), 200-4 (BCM, aligned with ISO 22301).
Standard 200-2 defines three protection approaches: Basic (Basis-Absicherung), Standard (Standard-Absicherung), Core (Kern-Absicherung).
IT-Grundschutz Compendium (updated annually): building blocks (Bausteine) across 10 process layers — ISMS, ORP, CON, OPS, DER, APP, SYS, IND, NET, INF.
Each building block specifies elementary threats and requirements at three levels: Basic, Standard and High Protection.
Cross-references to ISO/IEC 27001 controls and other standards included in each building block.
Standard Protection approach is equivalent to ISO/IEC 27001 certification.

name: Bankaufsichtliche Anforderungen an die IT
description: Supervisory requirements for IT in banks issued by the German Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (BaFin). BAIT defines binding requirements for IT governance, IT risk management, information security, IT projects and outsourcing for German credit institutions under § 25a KWG.
businessImpact: Mandatory for all German credit institutions (banks, savings banks, cooperative banks) under § 25a KWG.
Specifies binding requirements for IT governance, IT risk management, information security management and IT operations.
Non-compliance can trigger supervisory measures including fines, remediation orders or management restrictions by BaFin.
Revised in 2021 to add requirements for critical infrastructure protection (KRITIS alignment) and cloud outsourcing.
Organisations must appoint an Information Security Officer (ISB) independent of IT operations.
Regular self-assessments and reporting to senior management on IT risk and information security posture required.
structure: 12 chapters covering the full IT governance and security lifecycle.
Ch. 1: IT Strategy — board-level IT governance and risk appetite.
Ch. 2: IT Governance — structures, roles (ISB, IT risk manager), committees.
Ch. 3: Information Risk Management — risk identification, assessment, treatment and reporting.
Ch. 4: Information Security Management — ISMS, security policies, awareness.
Ch. 5: IT Operations — capacity management, availability, configuration and change management.
Ch. 6: IT Projects and Applications — secure development and project oversight.
Ch. 7: IT Outsourcing & Third-Party Management — EBA Guidelines alignment.
Ch. 8–12: Critical infrastructure protection, payment transactions, network security, emergency management, IT-related audit.

name: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard
description: Global security standard for organisations that store, process or transmit payment card data. Maintained by the PCI Security Standards Council (PCI SSC) and mandated by card brands (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover, JCB).
businessImpact: Non-compliance can result in card brand fines ($5k–$100k/month), increased transaction fees or loss of card acceptance rights.
Applies to all entities that store, process or transmit cardholder data or sensitive authentication data.
Four merchant levels (based on annual transaction volume) determine the required validation method (QSA audit, SAQ or internal audit).
v4.0 introduced 64 new requirements (including enhanced phishing protection, MFA expansion and customised approach option).
Customised approach (new in v4.0) allows organisations to achieve security objectives through alternative controls with rigorous testing.
structure: 12 Requirements organised under 6 control objectives.
Objective 1 — Build and Maintain a Secure Network: Req. 1 (network security controls), Req. 2 (secure configurations).
Objective 2 — Protect Account Data: Req. 3 (protect stored data), Req. 4 (encrypt in transit).
Objective 3 — Maintain a Vulnerability Management Program: Req. 5 (anti-malware), Req. 6 (secure systems & software).
Objective 4 — Implement Strong Access Control: Req. 7 (access by need-to-know), Req. 8 (user authentication), Req. 9 (physical access).
Objective 5 — Monitor and Test Networks: Req. 10 (logging & monitoring), Req. 11 (security testing).
Objective 6 — Maintain an Information Security Policy: Req. 12 (security policy & programme).

name: Trusted Information Security Assessment Exchange
description: Information security assessment and exchange scheme for the automotive industry, based on the VDA Information Security Assessment (VDA ISA). Managed by ENX Association, TISAX allows automotive OEMs and suppliers to share assessment results without repeated audits.
businessImpact: De facto mandatory for automotive supply chain: most German OEMs (VW, BMW, Mercedes-Benz, Porsche) require TISAX certification from suppliers handling sensitive data.
Three assessment levels: AL1 (self-assessment, normal protection), AL2 (verified by auditor, high protection / prototype data), AL3 (on-site audit, very high protection / development parts).
Results shared via ENX portal; avoids duplicate audits across multiple OEM customers.
Prototype and development data categories require the highest AL3 level with mandatory on-site assessments.
Scope typically covers IT infrastructure, physical security, and information handling processes at relevant locations.
Non-certification can disqualify suppliers from OEM contracts involving sensitive information exchange.
structure: Based on VDA ISA (Information Security Assessment) questionnaire, aligned with ISO/IEC 27001.
6 domains in VDA ISA 6.x: Information Security, HR Security, Physical Security, Identity & Access Management, IT Security, Supplier Relationships.
Each requirement rated Must (mandatory), Should (recommended) or Can (optional); maturity rated 0–5.
Three assessment objectives: Information security (AL1/AL2), Prototype protection (AL2/AL3), Data connected car (AL2/AL3).
Assessment carried out by ENX-approved audit providers; results valid for 3 years.
Cross-referenced with ISO/IEC 27001, NIST CSF and IEC 62443 for industrial control systems.

name: Control Objectives for Information and Related Technologies
description: IT governance and management framework developed by ISACA. COBIT 2019 provides a comprehensive set of governance and management objectives to help enterprises achieve their IT-related goals while balancing benefits, risk and resources.
businessImpact: Widely adopted by enterprises and IT auditors; referenced by SOX, COSO ERM and internal audit standards.
Supports alignment of IT goals with business objectives and stakeholder needs through cascading goals.
40 governance and management objectives cover the full spectrum from board oversight to day-to-day IT operations.
Design factors (enterprise size, risk profile, sector, etc.) enable tailored framework implementation.
Recognised by ISACA for CGEIT and CISA certification requirements; used in IT audit engagements globally.
Version 2019 replaced COBIT 5 and introduced a redesigned performance management system (0–5 capability levels).
structure: Two domains: Governance of Enterprise IT (EDM — Evaluate, Direct and Monitor) and Management of Enterprise IT (APO, BAI, DSS, MEA).
5 governance objectives (EDM01–EDM05): Ensure governance framework, strategy delivery, benefits realisation, risk optimisation and resource optimisation.
35 management objectives across 4 domains: APO (Align, Plan and Organise), BAI (Build, Acquire and Implement), DSS (Deliver, Service and Support), MEA (Monitor, Evaluate and Assess).
Each objective has: purpose, goals, components (processes, org structures, information flows, people/skills, policies, culture, services/infrastructure).
Performance management: 6 capability levels (0–5) with defined attributes per level per objective.
Design Guide: 12 design factors used to tailor the framework scope and priorities to organisational context.

name: ISO/IEC 38500:2024
description: International standard for the governance of IT for organisations. Provides guiding principles for governing bodies on the effective, efficient and acceptable use of IT, applicable to organisations of all types and sizes.
businessImpact: Provides high-level governance principles applicable to boards of directors, executive teams and other governing bodies.
Complements COBIT and ITIL by providing the strategic governance context for IT management frameworks.
Supports compliance with corporate governance codes and regulations that require boards to address IT risk (e.g., King IV, NIS2 Art. 20).
Helps governing bodies fulfil accountability obligations for IT decisions, aligning IT with organisational strategy.
Widely referenced in national governance frameworks and used alongside sector-specific regulations.
structure: 3 core tasks for governing bodies: Evaluate (current and future IT use), Direct (preparation and implementation of plans), Monitor (IT performance and conformance).
6 principles for good governance of IT: Responsibility, Strategy, Acquisition, Performance, Conformance, Human Behaviour.
Model shows the relationship between governing body, management activities and IT-enabled business activities.
Principle 1 — Responsibility: Individuals and groups understand and accept their IT responsibilities.
Principle 2 — Strategy: IT strategy considers current and future IT capabilities and business strategy.
Principle 3 — Acquisition: IT acquisitions are made with due diligence and appropriate decision-making.
Principle 4 — Performance: IT is fit for purpose; supports organisational needs.
Principle 5 — Conformance: IT complies with mandatory legislation and regulations.
Principle 6 — Human Behaviour: IT policies respect human behaviour and needs.

name: ISO 31000:2018
description: International standard providing principles, framework and process for risk management applicable to any organisation regardless of type, size, activity or sector. Provides universal guidance for designing, implementing, maintaining and continually improving risk management.
businessImpact: Serves as the foundational risk management standard; referenced by ISO 27005, ISO 22301, ISO 9001, DORA and many sector frameworks.
Applicable to all types of risk (strategic, operational, financial, reputational, security) — not limited to IT or information security.
Supports alignment of risk management with organisational governance frameworks and objectives.
Widely used as the basis for enterprise risk management (ERM) programmes alongside COSO ERM.
Helps organisations comply with governance regulations that mandate documented risk management frameworks.
structure: 3 parts: Principles, Framework and Process — each interdependent and mutually reinforcing.
8 principles: integrated, structured & comprehensive, customised, inclusive, dynamic, best available information, human & cultural factors, continual improvement.
Framework components: Leadership & Commitment, Integration, Design, Implementation, Evaluation, Improvement.
Risk management process: Communication & Consultation → Scope/Context/Criteria → Risk Assessment (identification, analysis, evaluation) → Risk Treatment → Monitoring & Review → Recording & Reporting.
Risk assessment sub-process: identify risks, analyse likelihood and consequence, evaluate against risk criteria to determine treatment priority.
Annex (informative): attributes of enhanced risk management for mature organisations.

name: ITIL v3 (2011 Edition)
description: IT Infrastructure Library version 3 — a widely adopted framework of best practices for IT service management (ITSM). Organised around a service lifecycle model covering strategy, design, transition, operation and continual improvement.
businessImpact: Globally recognised ITSM framework; basis for hundreds of thousands of ITIL certifications worldwide.
Adoption typically reduces unplanned IT downtime, improves change success rates and lowers service desk costs.
Provides the operational process layer complementing governance frameworks (COBIT, ISO 38500).
ITIL v3 Foundation certification remains one of the most widely held IT certifications globally.
Many regulators and standards (NIS2, DORA, KRITIS) implicitly require ITIL-aligned practices for incident and change management.
structure: Service Lifecycle: 5 core publications — Service Strategy, Service Design, Service Transition, Service Operation, Continual Service Improvement.
Service Strategy: strategy management, service portfolio management, financial management, demand management, business relationship management.
Service Design: service level management, availability management, capacity management, IT service continuity, information security management, supplier management, design coordination.
Service Transition: change management, service asset and configuration management, release and deployment management, knowledge management, transition planning.
Service Operation: incident management, problem management, event management, request fulfilment, access management.
Continual Service Improvement: 7-step improvement process, service measurement, reporting.

name: ITIL 4
description: IT Infrastructure Library version 4 — the latest evolution of the ITIL framework, published by Axelos in 2019. Introduces the Service Value System (SVS) and 34 management practices, integrating ITSM with Agile, DevOps and Lean approaches.
businessImpact: Replaces ITIL v3 lifecycle model with a flexible Service Value System (SVS) suited to Agile and DevOps environments.
34 management practices (replacing ITIL v3 processes) give organisations modular, fit-for-purpose ITSM guidance.
ITIL 4 Foundation is the current baseline certification; ITIL Strategic Leader and Managing Professional tracks added.
Explicitly integrates with Agile, Lean, DevOps and cloud delivery models — addresses modern digital delivery.
Supports digital transformation programmes by embedding service management thinking in product and platform teams.
Widely required or referenced in IT outsourcing contracts and service provider capability assessments.
structure: Service Value System (SVS): Guiding Principles → Governance → Service Value Chain → Practices → Continual Improvement.
7 Guiding Principles: Focus on value, Start where you are, Progress iteratively, Collaborate, Think and work holistically, Keep it simple, Optimise and automate.
Service Value Chain: Plan, Improve, Engage, Design & Transition, Obtain/Build, Deliver & Support.
34 Practices across 3 categories: General Management (14), Service Management (17), Technical Management (3).
General Management practices include risk management, information security management, knowledge management and portfolio management.
Service Management practices include incident management, problem management, change enablement, service level management and more.
